idea: Plataforma de telemedicina con IA generativa para diagnostico preliminar y triage de salud mental en adolescentes en Latinoamerica

problem.summary: 16M+ adolescents with mental disorders in LATAM, 70%+ treatment gap, high pain intensity, 7 paid alternatives

market.summary: $452M LATAM mental health apps TAM, 14.6% CAGR, SOM $10.6M-$21.2M

competitive.summary: 12+ competitors with dominant incumbents (BetterHelp/Talkspace); 7 market gaps in LATAM teen AI triage

bizmodel.summary: LTV/CAC 5.7x, payback 2.2 months, $29/mo subscription validated by 6 precedents

risk.summary: High risk: multi-jurisdiction regulatory complexity (5/25), AI provider dependency (3/25), active litigation wave

key_strengths: Strong business model fundamentals (79/100): LTV/CAC ratio of 5.7x with 2.2-month payback period, validated by 6 comparable companies including Talkspace and Terapify — even worst-case sensitivity (price -20%) yields viable 4.56x ratio

High pain intensity with validated demand (75/100): 16M+ adolescents with diagnosed mental disorders in LATAM, 70%+ treatment gap, only 3.4 psychiatrists per 100K people, and 7 paid alternatives confirming willingness to pay up to $100/week
key_concerns: Severe regulatory risk (39/100, regulatory sub-score 5/25): Multi-jurisdiction AI + healthcare + minors regulation across LATAM with no harmonized framework — Woebot ($123M raised) and Yara AI both shut down due to regulatory barriers in this exact space; 7 wrongful death lawsuits against OpenAI for AI chatbot-related teen harm

Dominant incumbent entrenchment (competitive incumbent sub-score 3/20): BetterHelp/Teladoc ($950M segment revenue, NYSE-listed) and Talkspace ($800M+ acquisition) control the market — competing requires differentiation beyond pricing
critical_assumptions: A1: ARPU $29/mo based on LATAM competitive benchmark mid-to-high range — not directly validated for AI triage in teen mental health (BizModel)
A2: Monthly churn 8% estimated between subscription consumer 5-10% and Headspace 13% — no LATAM teen mental health churn benchmark exists (BizModel)
A3: Gross margin 55% assumes AI triage reduces therapist cost vs Talkspace 43-48% — unvalidated (BizModel)
A4: CAC $35 estimated for LATAM digital health — between $1 mobile baseline and $178 pharma benchmark, wide range (BizModel)
A5: SOM $10.6M-$21.2M is an estimate derived from TAM percentages, not bottom-up calculation (Market flag: som-is-estimate)

pivot_suggestions.direction: Pivot from AI diagnostic triage to AI-powered therapist copilot — provide AI tools to licensed LATAM mental health professionals rather than direct-to-teen AI interaction
pivot_suggestions.addresses: Risk score 39/100 (regulatory 5/25, dependency 3/25) — removes AI-to-minor interaction liability, avoids medical device classification, eliminates wrongful death lawsuit exposure
pivot_suggestions.revalidation_idea: Plataforma B2B de copiloto IA para psicologos y psiquiatras en Latinoamerica que atienden adolescentes — automatiza notas clinicas, sugiere intervenciones basadas en evidencia, y gestiona triage de citas

pivot_suggestions.direction: Target a single-country launch in Colombia or Mexico with B2G model (government/NGO contracts) instead of multi-jurisdiction B2C — mirrors Kooth's $680M California model
pivot_suggestions.addresses: Risk score 39/100 (regulatory fragmentation) and Competitive incumbent weakness (3/20) — B2G sidesteps consumer competition and simplifies regulatory compliance to one jurisdiction
pivot_suggestions.revalidation_idea: Plataforma de salud mental digital para adolescentes contratada por el gobierno de Colombia/Mexico — triage digital con supervision profesional integrada al sistema publico de salud

pivot_suggestions.direction: Remove AI generative diagnosis entirely and build a human-therapist marketplace with LATAM-affordable pricing ($15-25/session) — add AI only for matching/scheduling, not clinical functions
pivot_suggestions.addresses: Risk score 39/100 (all 3 top killers involve AI regulation/liability) and Competitive gap evidence (17/20 for Spanish-language teen-specific platforms)
pivot_suggestions.revalidation_idea: Marketplace de terapia online para adolescentes en Latinoamerica con precios accesibles ($15-25/sesion) — matching inteligente de terapeutas hispanohablantes especializados en jovenes

next_steps.action: Consult with LATAM healthtech regulatory counsel in Mexico or Colombia to determine if AI triage can be classified as a wellness tool (not medical device), avoiding the SaMD regulatory pathway that killed Woebot
next_steps.rationale: Regulatory risk (5/25 sub-score) is the single biggest blocker — if AI triage requires medical device classification in all target countries, the idea is unviable as structured

next_steps.action: Run a Spanish-language landing page test targeting parents of LATAM adolescents via Instagram/TikTok ads in Mexico and Colombia, measuring waitlist signups at $29/mo price point over 2 weeks
next_steps.rationale: Validates real demand from paying parents and tests LATAM-affordable pricing — addresses SOM estimate uncertainty

next_steps.action: Interview 10-15 licensed psychologists/psychiatrists in Mexico and Colombia about willingness to supervise AI triage and provide teleconsultations at $15-20/session therapist payout
next_steps.rationale: With only 3.4 psychiatrists per 100K in LATAM, validates supply-side feasibility for the human supervision component

next_steps.action: Evaluate the therapist-copilot pivot: if regulatory counsel indicates medical device classification is required, pivot analysis to B2B AI tools for mental health professionals instead of direct-to-teen AI
next_steps.rationale: The copilot pivot preserves the core AI differentiation while eliminating the three top risk killers (regulatory, liability, AI provider restrictions)

validation_experiments.experiment: Regulatory feasibility assessment with healthtech legal counsel in Mexico (COFEPRIS) and Colombia (INVIMA) to classify the AI triage component
validation_experiments.success_metric: At least 1 target country where AI triage can launch as wellness/non-medical-device with written legal opinion within 30 days
validation_experiments.what_it_validates: Whether the regulatory barrier (5/25 sub-score) is surmountable — Woebot and Yara AI failed at this exact point

validation_experiments.experiment: Landing page + waitlist test targeting LATAM parents via Instagram and TikTok ads in Spanish at $29/mo, $500 ad spend over 14 days
validation_experiments.success_metric: >150 waitlist signups (>$3.33 CPL) with >30% providing payment intent
validation_experiments.what_it_validates: Real consumer demand and price sensitivity at $29/mo — addresses SOM estimate uncertainty and parent willingness to pay

validation_experiments.experiment: Interview 10-15 licensed mental health professionals in Mexico/Colombia about platform participation at $15-20/session payout
validation_experiments.success_metric: >60% willingness to participate; >40% would start within 3 months
validation_experiments.what_it_validates: Supply-side feasibility given LATAM's 3.4 psychiatrists per 100K shortage

department_summaries.problem: Problem strongly validated: 16M+ adolescents in LATAM have diagnosed mental disorders with a 70%+ treatment gap, only 3.4 psychiatrists per 100K people, and multiple paid alternatives confirm willingness to pay — score 75/100 (high pain).
department_summaries.market: LATAM mental health apps market valued at $452M (2024) growing at 14.6% CAGR to $1.06B by 2030; conservative SOM of $10.6M-$21.2M for AI-powered adolescent triage with 3 identifiable early adopter segments — score 68/100 (moderate opportunity).
department_summaries.competitive: Validated market with 12+ competitors (BetterHelp, Talkspace, Brightline, Kooth globally; Terapify, Sanarai, Mindy in LATAM) but dominated by well-funded US incumbents; major gaps in Spanish-language teen-specific AI triage and LATAM-affordable pricing create a defensible niche — score 62/100.
department_summaries.bizmodel: Subscription model at $29/month yields strong unit economics (LTV/CAC 5.7x, payback 2.2 months) validated by 6 model precedents, though CAC and gross margin assumptions are estimated from adjacent benchmarks — score 79/100.
department_summaries.risk: High overall risk (score 39/100) driven by severe regulatory complexity across AI + healthcare + minors + multi-jurisdiction LATAM (5/25 regulatory sub-score), with 3 failed AI mental health predecessors confirming regulatory barriers as idea-killers.
